annotations.label: Scalar field

anchor.snippet: A scalar field smoothly assigns a scalar value to every point in a space

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated 'scalar field' definition; scalar fields appear only as plain functions `E → ℝ` (e.g. as inputs to `gradient`).

annotations.label: Vector field

anchor.snippet: A vector field is a smooth assignment of a vector to each point in a space

annotations.note: Vector fields are represented as functions `V : E → E` (the `VectorField` namespace) or sections `Π x, TangentSpace I x`, but there is no single dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Pseudovector and pseudoscalar fields

anchor.snippet: one further distinguishes pseudovector fields and pseudoscalar fields

annotations.note: No formalization of pseudovector/pseudoscalar fields or the orientation-reversal sign behavior that defines them.

annotations.label: Vector algebra

anchor.snippet: The algebraic (non-differential) operations in vector calculus are referred to as vector algebra

annotations.note: The core vector-algebra operations are formalized: cross product (`crossProduct`/`⨯₃`) and dot product (`Matrix.dotProduct`/`⬝ᵥ`).

annotations.label: Triple products

anchor.snippet: Also commonly used are the two triple products

annotations.note: The scalar triple product (`triple_product_eq_det`) and vector triple product (`cross_cross_eq_smul_sub_smul`) are both formalized.

annotations.label: Differential operators (del)

anchor.snippet: Vector calculus studies various differential operators defined on scalar or vector fields

annotations.note: The gradient is defined as a named operator, but divergence and curl are not defined as standalone operators (only a divergence theorem exists).

annotations.label: Laplace operators

anchor.snippet: Also commonly used are the two Laplace operators

annotations.note: The scalar Laplacian `Δ` (`laplacianWithin`/`Laplacian.laplacian`) is formalized, but the vector Laplacian is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Jacobian matrix

anchor.snippet: A quantity called the Jacobian matrix is useful for studying functions

annotations.note: The total derivative `fderiv` generalizes the Jacobian matrix and the Jacobian determinant appears in `MeasureTheory.Function.Jacobian`, but there is no dedicated Jacobian-matrix-of-partials definition.

annotations.label: Integral theorems

anchor.snippet: The three basic vector operators have corresponding theorems which generalize the fundamental theorem of calculus

annotations.note: The fundamental theorem of calculus and the divergence theorem are formalized, but the gradient theorem and Kelvin–Stokes theorem are not.

annotations.label: Green's theorem

anchor.snippet: the divergence and curl theorems reduce to the Green's theorem

annotations.note: The planar divergence theorem on rectangles is exactly the divergence form of Green's theorem.

annotations.label: Linear approximation formula

anchor.snippet: one can approximate f ( x , y ) for ( x , y ) close to ( a , b ) by the formula

annotations.note: `HasFDerivAt` encodes precisely f(x)=f(a)+f'(a)(x−a)+o(‖x−a‖), the first-order linear approximation, for general normed spaces.

annotations.label: Tangent plane

anchor.snippet: The right-hand side is the equation of the plane tangent to the graph

annotations.note: The affine approximation defining the tangent plane is given by `fderiv`/`HasFDerivAt`, but there is no named 'tangent plane to a graph' object.

annotations.label: Critical point

anchor.snippet: is critical if all of the partial derivatives of the function are zero

annotations.note: There is no `IsCritical` definition; critical points are expressed inline as the hypothesis `fderiv ℝ f a = 0`.

annotations.label: Classification of critical points

anchor.snippet: a critical point may be either a local maximum

annotations.note: `IsLocalMax`/`IsLocalMin` exist, but there is no saddle-point classification or second-derivative (Hessian) test for critical points.

annotations.label: Fermat's theorem

anchor.snippet: By Fermat's theorem , all local maxima and minima of a differentiable function occur at critical points

annotations.note: `IsLocalExtr.fderiv_eq_zero` (with `IsLocalMin`/`IsLocalMax` variants) states that local extrema have vanishing derivative.

annotations.label: Structure of Euclidean 3-space

anchor.snippet: Vector calculus is initially defined for Euclidean 3-space

annotations.note: Euclidean 3-space is `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)`, carrying the inner product/distance structure (with `Orientation` available).

annotations.label: Operators and required structure

anchor.snippet: The gradient and divergence require only the inner product

annotations.note: This meta-statement about which geometric structure each operator requires is not formalized.

annotations.label: Vector calculus on real vector spaces

anchor.snippet: Vector calculus can be defined on other 3-dimensional real vector spaces if they have an inner product

annotations.note: No formalization of vector calculus operators transported to general 3-dimensional inner-product spaces with orientation.

annotations.label: Vector calculus on Riemannian manifolds

anchor.snippet: More generally, vector calculus can be defined on any 3-dimensional oriented Riemannian manifold

annotations.note: Riemannian-manifold infrastructure exists, but grad/div/curl are not defined on oriented Riemannian 3-manifolds.

annotations.label: Generalization of grad and div

anchor.snippet: Grad and div generalize immediately to other dimensions

annotations.note: `gradient` is already defined for general inner-product spaces and the divergence theorem holds in ℝⁿ, but divergence is not packaged as a general named operator.

annotations.label: Fields as k-vector fields

anchor.snippet: the various fields in (3-dimensional) vector calculus are uniformly seen as being

annotations.note: The graded exterior algebra (k-vectors) is formalized, but the uniform identification of vector-calculus fields with k-vector fields is not.

annotations.label: Curl and cross product in dimension 3 or 7

anchor.snippet: grad of a scalar function is a vector field, and div of a vector field is a scalar function

annotations.note: Only the 3-dimensional cross product is formalized; the 7-dimensional cross product and the result that bilinear cross products exist only in dimensions 3 and 7 are not.

annotations.label: Geometric algebra generalization

anchor.snippet: The first, geometric algebra , uses k -vector fields instead of vector fields

annotations.note: Geometric (Clifford) algebra and exterior algebra are formalized, but they are not developed as a generalization of vector calculus via k-vector fields.

annotations.label: Differential forms and Stokes' theorem

anchor.snippet: The second generalization uses differential forms

annotations.note: The exterior derivative `extDeriv` of differential forms is defined, but the general Stokes' theorem is not formalized (only the divergence theorem on boxes).

annotations.label: Vector field as map to tangent bundle

anchor.snippet: a vector field is defined as a map from a manifold

annotations.note: The tangent bundle `TangentBundle I M` is defined and vector fields are taken as sections `Π x, TangentSpace I x`.

annotations.label: Covector field

anchor.snippet: a covector field is a map from a manifold to its cotangent bundle

annotations.note: Covector fields appear pointwise as `Π x, TangentSpace I x →L[𝕜] 𝕜` (e.g. `extDerivFun`), but there is no dedicated cotangent bundle type.
